passages.text: I went to the woods because I wished to live deliberately, to front only the essential facts of life, and see if I could not learn what it had to teach, and not, when I came to die, discover that I had not lived.
But I am very poorly today and very stupid and hate everybody and everything.
Depend upon it, sir, when a man knows he is to be hanged in a fortnight, it concentrates his mind wonderfully.
passages.source: Samuel Johnson, quoted in Boswell's Life of Johnson, 1777

passages.text: Great God! this is an awful place and terrible enough for us to have laboured to it without the reward of priority.
passages.source: Capt. Robert Falcon Scott's diary at the South Pole, 17 January 1912

passages.text: It seems a pity, but I do not think I can write more. For God's sake look after our people.
passages.source: Scott's final diary entry, Antarctica, March 1912

passages.text: Delight itself, however, is a weak term to express the feelings of a naturalist who, for the first time, has wandered by himself in a Brazilian forest.
We saw the fire grow in a most horrid malicious bloody flame, not like the fine flame of an ordinary fire. It made me weep to see it.
passages.source: Samuel Pepys on the Great Fire of London, 2 September 1666 (adapted)

passages.text: If I do not return, my dear Sarah, never forget how much I love you, nor that, when my last breath escapes me on the battlefield, it will whisper your name.
passages.source: Maj. Sullivan Ballou to his wife, 14 July 1861 (adapted)

passages.text: I hope this email finds you well! I just wanted to circle back and touch base regarding our shared synergies. Let's not just think outside the box — let's redefine it. Please don't hesitate to reach out!
passages.tell: It found you well, it circled back, and it touched base — the full corporate séance in four sentences.

passages.text: Today I delved into the rich tapestry of my morning routine. It wasn't just coffee — it was a journey. In today's fast-paced world, taking a moment to truly pause is nothing short of a game-changer.
passages.tell: 'Delve' and 'rich tapestry' in one entry — the machine showed its whole hand.

passages.text: Unpopular opinion: kindness is free. ✨ Let that sink in. It's not just a mindset — it's a movement. Who's with me? 💪 #Blessed #GrowthMindset
passages.tell: Inspiration-bot bingo: a sparkle emoji, 'let that sink in', and a hashtag prayer.

passages.text: Success isn't a destination — it's a journey. And the journey? It's not about the miles — it's about the moments. Read that again.
passages.tell: Em dashes wholesale, plus 'Read that again' — the engagement-bait sign-off.

passages.text: Weeknight pasta for when the day won. Fifteen minutes, one pan, and the parmesan rind you forgot you saved doing most of the heavy lifting.
passages.tell: Cozy and competent, but engineered — 'for when the day won' is a headline, not a memory.

passages.text: The library's roof fund reached its goal Tuesday after an anonymous donor covered the final $4,200, ending a drive that began with a bake sale in March.
passages.tell: Almost perfect wire copy — but every number resolves too neatly, like a story writing itself backward.

passages.text: Dear Sam — the seeds you sent came up in rows so straight I suspect them of ambition. Write when the harvest lets you.
passages.tell: Charming, but the wit is load-bearing — real farm letters spend more words on weather and debt.

passages.text: Camped at last beneath the great ice barrier. The dogs are failing and the biscuit is short, yet I cannot bring myself to despair of the Pole. Tomorrow we shall know whether Providence intends us for glory or for graves.
passages.tell: Too rounded — real polar diaries log depots, mileage and frostbite, not matched pairs like “glory or graves.”

passages.text: Up early, and to the river, where I did see the flames advancing most terribly upon the warehouses. Lord, what confusion of carts and crying children. And so home, much troubled in mind, and to bed.
passages.tell: A greatest-hits reel of Pepys signatures — “up early,” “Lord!,” “and so to bed” — all in four lines.

passages.text: The pass was reported impassable, which I took, as usual, for an invitation. My pony disagreed at length and in Icelandic. We reached the hut long after dark, frozen quite through, and I have seldom been happier.
passages.tell: Every sentence lands a punchline. Real Victorian travelers let some weather just be weather.
